Bulle

FRFribourg

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The fire that made a star

A child watches Bulle burn on the night of 2 April 1805, as flames consume the medieval wooden houses in hours. From the ashes, a mysterious architect appears with a star-shaped plan, and together they walk the wide new streets, each one pointing toward a different valley and a different story of rebuilding.

Create This Story

On 2 April 1805, an enormous fire swept through Bulle and destroyed almost the entire town in a few hours -- the greatest fire in the history of the Canton of Fribourg. More than a thousand inhabitants were ruined. An outpouring of solidarity from the cantons of Zurich, Vaud, Bern, Neuchatel, Valais, and Geneva funded the relief effort. The reconstruction lasted half a century and transformed Bulle: wide, straight streets radiated from a new central square, giving the town a distinctive star shape with unified neoclassical facades.

The bishop's castle and the count's castle

Two rival castles -- Bulle's bishop's fortress and the Counts' hilltop stronghold at Gruyeres -- have argued across the valley for centuries about who truly rules La Gruyere. A child must carry a wheel of Gruyere cheese from one to the other to broker peace between the bishop's keep and the count's tower, discovering the tangled history of power along the way.

Create This Story

The Chateau de Bulle was built starting in 1291 under Bishop Guillaume de Champvent to defend against the rival Counts of Gruyere and of Savoy. Its donjon rises 33 meters and follows a carre savoyard plan. Just 5 km away, the Chateau de Gruyeres was built between 1270 and 1282 as the seat of the Counts of Gruyere, who ruled for nineteen generations. In 1554, the last count, Michel de Gruyere, went bankrupt, and his lands were divided between the cantons of Fribourg and Bern.

The poya that came alive

A child touches a poya painting on a farmhouse wall near Bulle, and the painted cows step off the facade. Bells clanging and herders calling, the procession winds up the mountain trail toward the high pasture. The child must join the march and lead the herd to the alpage before the last bell stops ringing at sunset.

Create This Story

In the Fribourg dialect, 'poya' describes the ritual montee a l'alpage -- the seasonal driving of cattle up to the high mountain pastures. Since the early 19th century, paintings depicting this tradition have adorned farmhouse facades in the Gruyere region. Sylvestre Pidoux (1800-1871), born in Vuadens near Bulle, is considered the first poya painter; his compositions from around 1850 set the enduring template. Approximately 800 poyas have been counted in the Gruyere region, with about fifteen active painters still creating them today.

Six hours at the Benichon table

At a magical Benichon feast, each course transports a child to a different scene from the Gruyere countryside: the saffron cuchaule takes them to a golden wheat field, the poires a Botzi to an autumn orchard heavy with fruit, and the meringue to a snow-covered alp where cows graze in summer. They must taste every dish to find the way home.

Create This Story

The Benichon (from Latin benedictio; German: Kilbi) is a centuries-old harvest festival unique to the Canton of Fribourg, considered by many Fribourgeois as more important than Christmas. The traditional menu is a legendary feast lasting six hours or more, featuring cuchaule (a golden saffron brioche), moutarde de Benichon (a sweet-spicy spread with vin cuit and star anise), lamb stew with raisins, poires a Botzi (small pears poached in spiced wine syrup), bricelets (thin wafer cookies), and meringues with double cream. In the lowlands including Bulle, the Benichon falls on the second Sunday of September.

Chenaux's march

One moonlit night, the bronze statue of Pierre-Nicolas Chenaux comes alive in the centre of Bulle and asks a child to help him finish the march to Fribourg he never completed. Together they walk through two centuries of Gruyere history, meeting farmers, soldiers, and dreamers, learning why the rebel's vision of freedom eventually came true.

Create This Story

Pierre-Nicolas Chenaux (1740-1781), born in La Tour-de-Treme near Bulle, led the most significant popular revolt in the history of the Canton of Fribourg. On 29 April 1781, he assembled some 2,500 men at the Auberge de l'Epee Couronnee in Bulle and marched on the city of Fribourg to challenge the patrician oligarchy. Bern sent cavalry and artillery to rout the insurgents. Chenaux was captured and executed on 5 May 1781. On 24 September 1933, a bronze statue by sculptor Carl Angst was erected in Bulle. Chenaux is honored as a precursor of the democratic movements that transformed Switzerland in the 19th century.

The organ that echoes from the narthex

A child sneaks into the Church of Saint-Pierre-aux-Liens at night and touches a key of the Mooser organ. The Echo keyboard answers from across the nave, and two ghostly voices emerge -- the organ builder Aloys Mooser and the bell caster who melted cannons into bells -- telling the story of a town that rebuilt itself from silence into song.

Create This Story

The Church of Saint-Pierre-aux-Liens in Bulle was rebuilt after the devastating fire of 1805 and consecrated on 22 September 1816. Its great organ was built by Aloys Mooser of Fribourg, the same builder who created the celebrated organ in Fribourg's Cathedrale Saint-Nicolas. Mooser added an innovative Echo keyboard whose pipes speak from the narthex, creating a striking effect of spatial distance that was widely imitated. Two cannons purchased from the State of Fribourg were melted down to cast the church's six bells. The organ was restored by Hans Fuglister in 1976.

The summit of Le Moleson

A child rides the funicular and cable car to the 2,002-meter summit of Le Moleson, where the 360-degree panorama comes alive: each mountain range tells a story -- the Bernese Alps whisper about glaciers, Mont Blanc about adventurers, the Jura about ancient seas. The child must remember all the stories before the clouds roll in and the summit falls silent.

Create This Story

Le Moleson (2,002 m) is the emblematic mountain of the Gruyere region, visible from Bulle. It is accessible by funicular from Moleson-sur-Gruyeres to Plan-Francey (1,520 m) and then by aerial cableway to the summit, where a 360-degree panorama reveals the Bernese Alps, Lake Geneva, Mont Blanc, and the Jura. Bulle sits at 770 meters at the foot of the Fribourg Pre-Alps, serving as the capital of the Gruyere district and the second-largest town in the Canton of Fribourg.

The last count's cheese

Count Michel de Gruyere, bankrupt and desperate in 1554, hides one final treasure: not gold, but the secret recipe for the perfect wheel of Gruyere. A child must follow clues from the castle cellars through the aging caves, racing against the bailiffs from Fribourg who are coming to seize everything the last count owned.

Create This Story

The production of cheese in the Gruyere region can be traced to 1115. The name 'Gruyere' became officially recognized in the 17th century and entered the Academie francaise dictionary in 1762. Gruyere received AOP status in 2011 at the European level. The last Count of Gruyere, Michel de Gruyere, faced lifelong financial difficulties and was declared bankrupt in 1554. His creditors, the cantons of Fribourg and Bern, divided his earldom. The cheese is matured in caves d'affinage for between 5 and 18 months.

Upload a photo
